topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a stark, unforgiving subject line. My hands, already clammy, began to tremble as I clicked it open. The words, formal and definitive, spoke of rejection. A hollow ache settled deep in my chest, a cold weight pressing down on my ribs. I reread the rejection letter three times, the ink blurring slightly under my frantic gaze. Every carefully crafted sentence that had poured my hopes into those pages now felt like a cruel mockery. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of the wood against the floor sounding deafening in the quiet dorm room. I didn't cry; the tears just pooled behind my eyes, hot and unwilling to escape, blurring the familiar posters on my wall into indistinct shapes.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the pixels swam before my eyes. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles strain beneath my skin. It wasn't just the words; it was the sudden, sickening drop in my stomach. I slowly closed the laptop, the click of the lid echoing like a final judgment. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the bustling campus below—students laughing, life moving forward—and felt utterly detached, as if I were watching a performance I was no longer part of. A strange, dull pressure built behind my eyes, making the world seem muted, distant. I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to anchor myself, but the air felt too thin, too sharp.

[story 3]

"No." The single word echoed in the silence of my small apartment, a flat, dead sound. I kept running my thumb over the corner of the rejection document, a repetitive, futile gesture. My shoulders slumped, a heavy, defeated weight settling onto my back. I felt a strange tightness across my throat, a physical constriction that made swallowing difficult. I stood up and walked to the kitchen, needing something tangible to focus on. I poured myself a glass of water, but the liquid felt cold and unsatisfying. Looking at my reflection in the dark glass, I saw only a pale, drawn face, and a profound sense of uselessness washed over me, leaving a residue of profound stillness.
